text: Mostra tuttiClimate change is primarily driven by human activities, especially the burning of fossil fuels like coal, oil, and gas. This releases heat-trapping greenhouse gases—such as carbon dioxide and methane—into the atmosphere, thickening the Earth's "blanket" and raising global temperatures. Welcome to the United Nations +1The main drivers fall into these categories: Energy & Electricity: Burning fossil fuels to generate electricity and heat buildings accounts for the largest share of global emissions.Transportation: Cars, trucks, ships, and planes run predominantly on fossil fuels, making the transport sector a massive contributor to global warming.Industry & Manufacturing: Producing goods like cement, steel, and plastics releases significant amounts of carbon dioxide and other industrial pollutants.Deforestation: Forests naturally absorb carbon dioxide. Cutting them down or clearing land diminishes this effect while releasing stored carbon back into the atmosphere.Agriculture: Intensive farming and livestock rearing (e.g., cows digesting food) emit large amounts of methane and nitrous oxide. Welcome to the United Nations +5To explore data-backed breakdowns and the latest scientific consensus, check out the NASA Science Causes of Climate Change overview or read the United Nations Climate Action guide.
